skill.name: chrome-extension-icons
skill.description: Generate Chrome extension icon PNGs from Iconify icons or local SVG files, then update manifest.json icon entries.
skill.summary: Create Chrome extension icon assets and manifest entries from Iconify or local SVG files.
skill.author: Boulea7

security_audit.summary: AI review did not confirm the static critical heuristic as malicious. Most backtick, weak-crypto, and hidden-file alerts are false positives from markdown fences, template literals, URLs, and the ~/.claude installation path. The real risk is medium because the skill runs a local Node script that downloads icons, reads SVG/config files, writes PNG files, and updates manifest.json paths.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Local File Reads and Writes
medium_findings.description: The generator accepts user-supplied input, output, manifest, and config paths. This is necessary for the skill, but it can read local SVG/config files and overwrite or create files if pointed at unintended paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and semantically confirmed, but they support the stated icon-generation workflow. Risk depends on the paths chosen by the user or assistant.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Executed Shell Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run local Node commands and dependency installation commands. This is expected for a generator skill, but users should review commands before execution, especially installation commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands are explicit and tied to the skill purpose. The sudo apt-get example raises operational risk, but no malicious command sequence was found.

low_findings.title: Network Access Limited to Iconify and Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The script uses HTTP modules and builds Iconify API URLs for search, SVG download, and preview links. No evidence found that it sends local files, credentials, or environment values over the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL construction and request helper are clear. The destinations match the documented Iconify workflow, with no observed exfiltration payload.

low_findings.title: Benign DEBUG Environment Check
low_findings.description: The only environment access found is process.env.DEBUG, used to decide whether to print an error stack. No evidence found of secret access or environment exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment reference is a single debug flag check in error handling. It does not read credential-like variables.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives from Markdown and Template Literals
low_findings.description: Many external command and weak-cryptography alerts are caused by markdown code fences, shell examples, URLs, icon identifiers, and JavaScript template literals rather than Ruby backticks or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed examples show prose and shell snippets, while JavaScript backticks are template literals. No Ruby shell execution or cryptographic implementation was present in these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Plus Filesystem Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines external downloads with local file creation and manifest updates. This combination is legitimate for icon generation but should be disclosed before marketplace publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combined behavior is directly present in the implementation. It is not malicious by itself, but it increases blast radius if paths or downloaded input are misused.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Dependency Installation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes an Ubuntu dependency fix using sudo apt-get. This should require explicit user consent because it changes system packages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is visible in troubleshooting documentation and appears legitimate for Sharp dependencies. The concern is privilege level, not malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Chrome extensions need several PNG icon sizes and correct manifest entries. This skill searches Iconify, converts SVG sources, and updates manifest.json for extension projects.
actual_capabilities: Searches Iconify for extension icon candidates by keyword.
Downloads selected SVG icons from Iconify API endpoints.
Converts SVG input into 16, 32, 48, and 128 pixel PNG files.
Applies a simple solid color replacement for many single-color SVG icons.
Updates manifest.json icons and action.default_icon entries when present.
Supports batch generation from a project configuration file.
limitations: Requires Node.js and the Sharp image library to run the generator script.
Needs network access for Iconify search and download workflows.
Color replacement is simple and may not preserve gradients or multi-color artwork.
Writes to user-supplied paths, so users should review target directories first.

use_cases.title: Prepare Store Assets
use_cases.description: Generate required icon sizes and manifest entries before packaging an extension.

use_cases.title: Apply Brand Colors
use_cases.description: Create simple single-color extension icons that match a product palette.

use_cases.title: Convert Existing Logos
use_cases.description: Convert an existing SVG logo into Chrome extension PNG files.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic Icon
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one extension icon.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a calendar icon for my Chrome extension and update manifest.json.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Brand Color
prompt_templates.prompt: Create Chrome extension icons for a home icon using the color #2563eb.

prompt_templates.title: Convert My SVG
prompt_templates.scenario: User has an existing logo file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert ./assets/logo.svg into Chrome extension icon sizes and place them in ./icons.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Multiple Projects
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate Chrome extension icons for every project in my batch configuration file.

output_examples.input: Generate a settings icon for my extension.
output_examples.output: The assistant presents an Iconify preview, asks for confirmation, generates four PNG sizes, and reports the updated manifest entries.

output_examples.input: Use my local logo SVG for the extension icon.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the SVG path, converts it into Chrome icon sizes, and summarizes the generated files.

output_examples.input: The first icon is too plain.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists alternative Iconify results with preview links and offers to regenerate the icon set.

best_practices: Preview the selected icon before generating files.
Confirm the manifest path and output directory before writing assets.
Use simple SVG artwork for the clearest small-size icons.
anti_patterns: Do not run the generator against paths outside the intended project.
Do not assume color replacement will preserve complex SVG artwork.
Do not install native dependencies without user approval.

faq.question: What icon sizes does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates 16, 32, 48, and 128 pixel PNG icons by default.

faq.question: Does it update manifest.json?
faq.answer: Yes. It can create or update the icons object and action.default_icon when present.

faq.question: Where do icons come from?
faq.answer: The search and generate workflow uses the public Iconify API.

faq.question: Can it use my own logo?
faq.answer: Yes. The convert workflow reads a local SVG file and outputs Chrome extension PNG sizes.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Internet access is needed for Iconify search and download. Local SVG conversion can work without Iconify.

faq.question: Is it safe to run on any project?
faq.answer: Run it only after checking the output and manifest paths because it writes files.
